title: Chapter 26: The Last Stand
content: Three days after the last facility fell, Patricia Holloway called Chloe directly.

Not through a PR firm. Not through a cable news interview. Not through a statement issued at 2 AM. A direct call, from a number Chloe didn't recognize, to the phone Chloe had been carrying since she'd walked out of Serenity five weeks ago.

"Chloe," Holloway said. Her voice was different — not the calibrated warmth she'd used in the Serenity lobby, not the compassionate-authority tone she'd used on cable news. Something underneath both. Something tired. "We need to talk."

Chloe was on the balcony of the Boulder Airbnb. Behind her, through the sliding glass door, twenty-three women were celebrating the final facility closures. Someone had brought champagne. Maya was explaining shot put trajectories to a group of volunteers from Texas. Jamie was on the phone with Grace from Mohawk Valley, coordinating aftercare for the discharged patients. Layla was already working on the legal discovery pipeline — the Alliance's communications, the DOJ's warrants, every piece of evidence that would be needed for the prosecutions ahead.
"So talk," Chloe said.
"Not on the phone. In person. Just you and me. No lawyers. No PR firms. No cameras."
"You spent six weeks calling me an anti-psychiatry extremist on national television. Why would I meet you in person?"
"Because I have something you want."

Chloe studied her. The woman who'd called her "sweetheart" while prescribing her olanzapine. The woman who'd stood in front of a camera on national television and called her an anti-psychiatry extremist. The woman who'd spent thirty years pathologizing women who refused to comply and was now, finally, sitting across from one of those women in a diner in Denver, asking for mercy.
"You want a deal," Chloe said.
"I want to not spend the rest of my life in prison."
"That's not the same thing as being sorry."
Holloway was quiet for a moment. "I know."

"Do you?" Chloe leaned forward. "Do you understand what you did? Not to me — I was there for fourteen days. I got out. I'm fine. But the women who were there for years? Rachel, who spent three years memorizing files because no one would believe her? Jamie, who spent six months hiding a phone in a fake plant and waiting for someone to come? Grace, who walked into a server room with a USB drive because she'd spent two years learning exactly when the IT contractor propped the door open? Do you understand what thirty years of 'compliance therapy' did to thousands of women?"
Holloway's jaw tightened. "I understand."
"No. You understand that you're going to lose. That's not the same as understanding what you did."
"What do you want me to say?"

"I want you to say nothing." Chloe pulled out the tablet. "I want you to give me the client registry. The full list. Every family, every payment, every facility, twelve years of admissions. And then I want you to testify. In open court. Against every family that paid to make their daughter disappear. Every facility director who knew the patients weren't clinically committable. Every lawyer who drafted the NDAs. Every PR consultant who helped you call us 'extremists' on national television."
Holloway was quiet for a long time. The Fleetwood Mac jukebox switched to "The Chain." Vasquez, across the room, didn't move — but her eyes never left Holloway's hands.
"If I do that," Holloway said, "I'll be a pariah. Every family. Every facility. Every institution I spent thirty years serving. They'll destroy me."

"They already destroyed you." Chloe's voice was calm — the same calm she'd used on Sterling in his office, the same calm she'd used on her father on the phone. "You just haven't admitted it yet. The Alliance has collapsed. The donors have fled. The lawyers have quit. The PR firm has dropped you. Your cable news interviews have stopped. Your statements have stopped being covered. You're already a pariah. The only question is whether you're a pariah in prison or a pariah who helped finish what she started."
"What I started?"

"You started the Alliance. You were the public face of the counter-organization. You called us extremists on national television. You coordinated with Cynthia Webb to protect Aurora. You warned the facilities we were coming." Chloe leaned back. "You built the wall. Now you can help us tear it down. Or you can go to prison knowing that the wall came down anyway and your name is on the wrong side of history."

Holloway looked at her. For the first time since Chloe had met her — since the dove-gray cardigan and the calibrated smile and the *trust me* / *obey me* handshake — something in Holloway's face moved that wasn't performance.
"You really think you can win," Holloway said.

"We already won." Chloe slid the tablet across the table. "The facilities are closed. The patients are free. The Alliance is dead. The only thing left is the paperwork. And the paperwork is your choice."
Holloway looked at the tablet. At the client registry template — a blank form, waiting to be filled with twelve years of names.
She reached for it.
